dialogue.utterance: Priority 1 for a stroke at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I fell, and my right ankle hurts a lot.
She tried to catch herself on the sink after the fall.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is Sarah.
Do you know where we are right now?
Yes, I'm at home.
Can you tell me the date?
February thirteenth.
And can you tell me what happened to bring you to 911?
I fell in the bathroom and hurt my ankle.
Ma'am, may we take a look at your airway to make sure you're breathing well?
Yes, go ahead.
Airway is clear and patent.
Let me check your breathing. Okay, it looks normal.
Your respirations are at 22 breaths a minute and regular right now.
I'm going to take your blood pressure now.
Blood pressure is 176 over 90.
Heart rate is 97 and regular.
Oxygen saturation is at 92% on room air.
Ma'am, can you tell me if you have any medical conditions or allergies?
I have diabetes, epilepsy, and had a stroke before.
And any allergies?
I am allergic to iodine or contrast dye.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take warfarin for my blood thinner needs.
You fell this morning-about when?
It happened between 6:30 and 7:30 this morning.
Did you hit your head when you fell?
No, I did not hit my head during the fall.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is your ankle pain right now? Please rate it for us.
Eight. It's painful, especially when I touch or move it.
Your skin appears pale, dry, but I don't see any cyanosis or mottling at this time.
Your head is normocephalic, and no visible neck abnormalities are present at this time.
Can we shine a light in your eyes to check your pupils? It's part of our routine exam to assess your neuro status. Is that okay with you?
Yes, go ahead.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light on both sides. That's reassuring.
Skin turgor is low, but only in the extremities. Still, nothing alarming here at the moment.
There's an obvious deformity in your right ankle. I'm going to support it properly while we analyze further. Does that feel okay to you?
FAST and VAN exams have been conducted and are both negative. No facial asymmetry or arm weakness is found, and your speech is slightly slurred but has been like this since after your stroke.
I see on record you have a history of insulin-dependent diabetes, epilepsy with seizures, and a prior stroke due to a clot. Is that correct?
Yes, that is all true. I've lived with all of these for a while.
Respirations are now at 23 breaths a minute and show no signs of distress or work of breathing visible at this point.
Oxygen saturation has decreased slightly to 87% right now. I'll start a nasal cannula for 2 liters per minute to help out with that. Better with that in place?
We're going to give you some fentanyl to help with the pain we discussed earlier. Is that okay with you, Sarah?
Preparing to run a 4-lead EKG now. Just need you to lie a little still for a few seconds.
We've administered 50 micrograms of fentanyl to help manage your pain. We'll check in again soon.
We're following Protocol 4-1 for general trauma because of the fall, the ankle deformity, and associated pain.
Your pulse is now 69-down slightly from before, and remains regular.
Respirations are at 23 right now and showing stable breathing at this point in time.
Blood pressure is up a bit at 176/95. That's hypertension as per your vital signs.
Blood glucose is currently at 161-noticeable but not too high within the normal EMS range for now.
A secondary dose of 50 mcg fentanyl has been administered in the back of the ambulance to keep your pain under control.
EKG is showing a sinus rhythm, nothing concerning is seen at this point and we'll continue monitoring that closely.
Heart rate has dropped to 67 and remains steady right now. That's a positive sign for your breathing and pain control.
Respirations are at 13 now-slowed and consistent, which typically could reflect that your oxygen levels are equalizing again.
Blood pressure is currently at 167/98, so it's trending lower, which is encouraging.
The EKG continues in sinus rhythm, and we're watching for any changes in rhythm while we complete transport.
Oxygen saturation is back up to 95% now. That's doing well with the 2 LPM nasal cannula, we'll keep that going.
Later on record, oxygen saturation is at 93% under transport movement. Kept stable and monitored carefully.
Pulse has dipped to 62 now, and that's still regular. We're keeping an eye on it for stability as we move forward.
Breathing is at 19-okay. A little more after splinting, but a positive sign overall in terms of comfort.
Blood pressure is now 143/77-that's definitely improving as we go.
Pulse is stable at 62 now, no irregularities noted.
Breathing at 17-still light, but that's likely because of the splinting we just completed.
Blood pressure is now 120/75-overall showing a positive improvement in your vitals.
We will now be carefully transporting you to VAMC Richmond via stretcher, following all protocol measures for safety.
You are now on the stretcher, with a splinted right ankle and we will carefully move you into the back of the ambulance.
Stretcher is now properly secured in the back of M78. Let's finish up the IV therapy and get you ready for the next steps in your care.
It's time to start an IV in your left arm to ensure we can give fluids and any necessary medications as needed.
Our first attempt was unsuccessful, but the second attempt worked. We've successfully placed an 18-gauge IV in your left arm.
Your ankle has been splinted with a rigid FrackCare splint to prevent further injury and provide stability during transport.
Glasgow Coma Scale or GCS is 15-Eye 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6. You're alert and oriented, doing great, Sarah.
We have now completed all treatments, and we're ready to move you safely to the hospital for further evaluation and care.
Upon arrival, we'll transfer you to Room 8 at VAMC. We'll provide a detailed report to the receiving nurse to ensure you get continued care.
